content.problemStatement:
"All the pain in the world will not help you find something that does not exist..."

You are Dastan, the great Prince of Persia!

Under the tunnels of Alamut, you have discovered a path ― a sequence of NNN tiles (numbered 111 through NNN) ahead of you. You are currently on tile 111. There is a signboard beside you, which says thus:


Stepping on tile iii (for each valid iii) activates a switch described by a binary string SiS_iSi​ with length NNN. When you fell through the tunnels onto tile 111, switch S1S_1S1​ got activated.
For each valid iii, when the switch SiS_iSi​ gets activated, then for each jjj (1≤j≤N)1 \le j \le N)1≤j≤N), if the jjj-th character of SiS_iSi​ is '0', tile jjj becomes unsafe to step on; otherwise (if the jjj-th character is '1'), tile jjj becomes safe to step on.
Step on an unsafe tile and you shall testify the fate of all men ― death!
You shall find that which you seek on reaching tile NNN. This tile must also be safe when you step on it.
Being a trained warrior, you are able to make jumps with length up to KKK tiles, **in both directions**. In other words, if you are currently at a tile iii, you may jump to a tile jjj if jjj is a valid tile and ∣i−j∣≤K|i-j| \le K∣i−j∣≤K.

You want to reach tile NNN as quickly as possible ― you want to minimise the number of jumps you need to make. Find this minimum number of jumps.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer T. Each test case contains two space-separated integers N and K. N lines follow. For each i (1 ≤ i ≤ N), the i-th of these lines contains a single string Si with length N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer — the minimum number of jumps you need to reach your target, or -1 if it is impossible.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Example case 1: The optimal sequence of jumps is 1→2→4→3→51→2→4→3→5. A shorter sequence does not exist.
Example case 2: There is no sequence of jumps that would allow you to reach tile 333.
Example case 3: The optimal sequence of jumps is 1→4→7→10.
Example case 4: There is no sequence of jumps that would allow you to reach tile 1010.

solution.approach: The problem can be solved using Breadth-First Search (BFS) on a graph. The graph has N nodes, each representing a tile. There is a directed edge from node i to node j if the absolute difference between i and j is less than or equal to K. The BFS traversal starts from node 1 and explores all reachable nodes. If the target node N is reachable, the minimum number of jumps is the distance from node 1 to node N. Otherwise, the answer is -1.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N * K) with K being the maximum jump length
